tagline: Lead UX Designer. Eight years across enterprise tools, mobile apps, and design systems.
Currently building through an AI-native design-to-code pipeline.

meta.location: Batumi, Georgia · open to EU/US remote

about.text: I help companies turn complex problems into clear, usable products. With experience across enterprise platforms, mobile apps, and web products, I lead the design process end-to-end — from user research and information architecture to prototyping and launch.

enterprise_design_system.title: Enterprise Design System
enterprise_design_system.hook: Three-tier tokens, two themes, one pipeline from Figma to production code.

meta.status: In production, used by the dev team

enterprise_design_system.stakes: Components shipped 1:1 with the design system on the first pass.

01_context.label: 01 — Context
01_context.heading: Context
body: The product this system was built for is an internal enterprise data platform — a place where engineers find, evaluate, and access data assets. The specifics are kept vague here by design. What mattered for the system: it needed two themes, a small but focused component set, and a direct path from design file to production code.

Off-the-shelf systems like Material or Fluent were not the right fit. They solve for breadth. This product needed depth in a specific interaction model, with a visual language that matched the platform's tone. A custom system also meant every token and component had a clear owner and a clear reason to exist.

I was the only designer. The system grew in parallel with the product — not before it. There was no separate design system phase. Every new screen was an opportunity to extract a pattern. Every extracted pattern reduced the cost of the next screen.

02_problem.label: 02 — The problem
02_problem.heading: The problem
02_problem.intro: Four things made early handoffs slow and error-prone.

cards.label: Handoff gap
cards.title: Screenshots in Jira
cards.body: Figma files were shared as static exports. Developers re-interpreted specs by eye. Documentation drifted as soon as the next design iteration started.

cards.label: Duplication
cards.title: Two files per component
cards.body: Light and dark mode were handled at the component level — copy the component, override the colors. Any fix required touching two places.

cards.label: No shared language
cards.title: Designers used layer names, devs used hex values
cards.body: There was no token layer connecting the two. Color changes in Figma did not propagate to code. Every update was a manual conversation.

cards.label: Reinvention
cards.title: Each screen rebuilt existing patterns
cards.body: The filter row, the card, the empty state — each re-solved on new screens without a shared reference. Inconsistencies accumulated faster than they could be caught.

03_approach.label: 03 — Approach
03_approach.heading: How the system grew
body: The system was never a separate project. There was no design system phase before the product phase. The two ran together from the start.

Every screen that was designed went through a two-step check: does this use a pattern that already exists in the system? If yes, use it. If no, does this pattern belong in the system? If yes, extract it. A pattern was promoted to the library only after it appeared in at least two separate product screens. No speculative components. No patterns that hadn't been used.

04_token_architecture.label: 04 — Token architecture
04_token_architecture.heading: Three tiers — Primitives, Semantic, Component
04_token_architecture.intro: Three tiers solve a problem that two tiers do not: you want components to be theme-aware without coupling component logic to raw color values. Two tiers almost works. Three tiers actually works, because the semantic layer acts as a stable interface between what changes (the theme) and what doesn't (the component).

tiers.name: Primitives
tiers.description: Raw values with no semantic meaning. Every color, spacing step, and type size in the system lives here. red-50, space-4, font-size-14. Primitives never appear in components directly.

tiers.name: Semantic
tiers.description: Intent-based tokens that map to a primitive based on the active theme. surface.primary, text.muted, border.default. In light mode, surface.primary resolves to one primitive. In dark mode, it resolves to another. Components never know which.

tiers.name: Component
tiers.description: Scoped to specific components. button.primary.background, card.border, input.text. These reference semantic tokens, never primitives. The component tier is what the MCP pipeline reads when it generates code.

05_naming.label: 05 — Naming
05_naming.heading: Naming was the unlock
body: The Figma MCP server reads the Figma file structure — layer names, variable names, component names — and exposes them as structured data the dev's IDE can query. If a token in Figma is called surface.primary and the same token in code is called color-bg-main, the pipeline produces a mismatch on every component.

The naming conventions were agreed on before the first component was built: tier prefix, semantic category, variant. That one decision turned a manual process into an automated one. It also meant token renames became breaking changes — which made the team careful about when and why names changed.

06_pipeline.label: 06 — Pipeline
06_pipeline.heading: From Figma to production code
06_pipeline.intro: The pipeline connects Figma to the developer's IDE through a Figma MCP server. A developer queries a component by node ID and gets back the structure, tokens, and variants. Claude Code generates the component from that output. The design file is the source of truth — not a screenshot of it.
before: Annotated screenshots attached to Jira tickets
Documentation goes stale between design iterations
Code reviewed against static exports, not live design
Token changes communicated in Slack, applied manually
after: Developer queries the Figma node directly from the IDE
Claude Code generates the component from live design data
Token updates in Figma propagate to the next build automatically
First-pass output matches the design system 1:1
No back-and-forth on spacing, color, or naming

mcp_operations.name: get_design_context
mcp_operations.description: Returns component structure, layout constraints, and variant data for a given node ID.

mcp_operations.name: get_metadata
mcp_operations.description: Returns layer name, type, visibility, and parent/child relationships.

mcp_operations.name: get_variable_defs
mcp_operations.description: Returns all token values resolved for the active theme — primitives, semantic, and component tiers.

06_pipeline.example_prompt: Using the Figma MCP server, read the design context for node [node-id] from file key 1yEn24FNbHfdrvdJdNT6fz. Generate the component in React using our existing token system. Match the Figma source 1:1.

07_themes.label: 07 — Themes
07_themes.heading: Two themes, one component
07_themes.body: Component tokens do not change between light and dark mode. Only the semantic layer resolves differently. button.primary.background always points to interactive.primary. In light mode, interactive.primary resolves to one primitive. In dark mode, it resolves to another. The component has no knowledge of which theme is active.

08_governance.label: 08 — Governance
08_governance.heading: Governance
08_governance.intro: The system is used in production. Changes have consequences. Four rules keep it stable.

rules.name: Naming
rules.rule: Every token follows the tier/category/variant convention. Variables added to Figma outside the convention are flagged before they're published. The same rules apply in code.

rules.name: Promotion
rules.rule: A pattern enters the system only after it has been used in at least two separate product screens. Local-first, then promoted. No hypothetical components.

rules.name: Propagation
rules.rule: Token updates move through the tier chain automatically. Changing a primitive updates all semantic tokens that reference it, which updates all component tokens in turn. The update surfaces in code on the next build.

rules.name: Changelog
rules.rule: Every system change ships with a changelog entry: token renamed, component prop added, variant deprecated. The changelog lives in the same repository as the production code.

stats.label: Components in system
stats.desc: Total components shipped and in active use

stats.label: Tokens across three tiers
stats.desc: Primitive, semantic, and component tokens combined

stats.label: Themes supported
stats.desc: Light and dark — one component definition each

stats.label: First-pass dev accuracy
stats.desc: Components matching design system on first implementation

stats.label: Handoff time saved
stats.desc: Per component, compared to previous screenshot-based process

columns.title: Where the system is heading
columns.body: The pipeline works. The next step is documentation good enough that a new engineer can use it without asking the designer. The MCP server connection, the Figma file key, the prompt structure — all of it needs a README that stands on its own. The goal is zero onboarding conversations needed.

columns.title: What I learned
columns.body: The token tier structure is right, but the semantic layer is still under-specified in places. Some component tokens skip semantic entirely and reference primitives directly — a shortcut that made sense early and now needs cleaning up. Building a system in parallel with a product teaches you which decisions age well and which ones don't. Naming ages well. Shortcuts don't.

halliburton.title: Data Marketplace
halliburton.hook: Shipping Halliburton's first unified data platform through an AI-native design-to-code pipeline.

meta.core_team: 3 engineers, PO, AI lead, Technology Director

halliburton.nda_notice: This project is under NDA. Sensitive details are blurred. General process and approach are shown.
halliburton.stakes: The first tool at Halliburton where engineers can find, trust, and access any data asset in minutes.

01_problem_context.label: 01 — Problem & Context
01_problem_context.heading: From days of searching to minutes of finding

cards.label: Problem
cards.title: Scattered and unfindable
cards.body: Data assets live across disconnected systems with inconsistent metadata and unclear ownership.

cards.label: Goal
cards.title: One entry point
cards.body: A unified marketplace for curated data, reports, models, and AI agents — part of Halliburton's Data Ribbon.

cards.label: Success
cards.title: Minutes, not days
cards.body: Engineers find, validate, and request access to any data asset in under a few minutes.

timeline.title: Discovery
timeline.desc: Aligned with the Technology Director and AI lead on vision, then defined two product phases — MVP and Trust/AI — so the design system could scale without rework.

timeline.title: Research
timeline.desc: Mapped 4 persona groups, 5 user flows, user journey maps, and the information architecture that later powered the MCP hand-off.

timeline.title: Prototype & Design System
timeline.desc: Started with AI-generated prototypes to validate structure fast, then built a scalable design system with reusable tokens and components.

timeline.title: Validation
timeline.desc: Two rounds of testing with real users — Data POD Leads and Product Owners. Caught blind spots before a single line of code was written.

timeline.title: Hand-off to Dev
timeline.desc: Connected dev team to Figma through an MCP server. Developers pulled specs, tokens, and components directly into their IDE — no screenshots, no outdated docs.

timeline.title: Post-launch Research
timeline.desc: Tracked adoption, task success, and friction points after launch. The platform keeps evolving based on real usage data.

03_ux_strategy.label: 03 — UX Strategy
03_ux_strategy.heading: Two phases of product evolution
03_ux_strategy.intro: UX strategy is a long-term plan that connects user insights, product vision, and business objectives into a coherent direction for designing and improving the user experience.

phase1.title: Phase 1 — MVP
phase1.intro: Phase 1 established the foundation — a single marketplace where engineers can find, trust, and access any data asset. The focus was on structure, clarity, and core workflows before layering in AI and social.
phase1.vision: Provide a user-friendly means of accessing curated, trusted data, reports, models, and AI Agents that are a part of Halliburton's Data Ribbon.
phase1.problem: Users cannot efficiently work with data because it is difficult to find, difficult to access, and not easy to compare across multiple sources.
phase1.success: Data engineers use the dashboard daily as the primary entry point to find, validate, request access, and consume data assets within minutes — not days or weeks.

persona_groups.title: Data Scientist / Engineer
persona_groups.desc: Browse data products, fast and relevant search, preview data, request & obtain access quickly.

persona_groups.title: Business User
persona_groups.desc: Find existing reports across BI tools that answer questions from trusted data.

persona_groups.title: Data Product Owner
persona_groups.desc: Manage data product info, approve/reject accesses, define level of access.

persona_groups.title: Data POD Lead
persona_groups.desc: Oversee data products across teams, monitor quality and access patterns.

ux_goals: Help users find the right data product quickly
Make data product cards and details easy to understand
Make access statuses and permissions clear
Differentiate View and Edit access levels clearly
Enable product owners to add and manage products with minimal friction
core_user_scenarios: Search and discover relevant data products
Filter and narrow down data products
Open dataset detail page and understand if it's usable
Request and get 1-click access directly from dataset page
ux_principles: Reduce cognitive load — engineers already drown in data; the UI shouldn't add noise.
Trust over decoration — every element earns its place; metadata, status, and lineage come first.
Speed as a feature — every flow optimised for 'minutes, not days.'
Desktop-first — users live in multi-window data environments.

phase2.title: Phase 2 — Trust, Social & AI
phase2.intro: Phase 2 transforms the marketplace from a browsing tool into a trust-driven, AI-powered discovery platform. Users describe what they need in plain language, see ranked results they can trust, and make access decisions with confidence.
phase2.vision: Transform the Data Marketplace from a browsing tool into an intelligent, trust-driven data discovery platform powered by AI.
phase2.problem: Users can find data but cannot judge if it's reliable, relevant, or safe to use. Discovery is manual, trust signals are absent, and social knowledge is invisible.
phase2.success: Engineers and business users describe what they need in plain language, receive ranked trusted results in seconds, and make access decisions confidently.
phase2.structural_change: The existing browse experience moves to a secondary role. A new AI-powered entry point takes its place — built around intent rather than navigation.
ux_goals: Reduce time to find the right data product from weeks to minutes
Give users confidence to trust data before requesting access
Make approval decisions faster for owners
Surface peer knowledge that currently lives only in people's heads
Support both technical and business user journeys

04_research.label: 04 — Research
04_research.heading: Understanding users and their journeys
04_research.intro: Deep research phase to map out who uses the platform, how they interact with it, and where the friction points are. 4 persona groups, detailed user flows, and end-to-end journey maps shaped every design decision.

personas.name: Data Scientist / Engineer
personas.who: Technical specialist working with Python, ML models, and data integrations.
personas.pain: Hard to find datasets across sources; long approval cycles; unclear ownership.

personas.name: Business User
personas.who: Analysts, PMs, and business stakeholders using BI tools.
personas.pain: Too much raw data, too few curated business-ready reports; hard to trust freshness.

personas.name: Data Product Owner
personas.who: Product manager responsible for governance and lifecycle of data products.
personas.pain: Fragmented ownership; too many manual approval steps; slow onboarding.

personas.name: Data POD Lead
personas.who: Team/Tech Lead for a data engineering POD or domain.
personas.pain: Heavy manual documentation work; poor visibility into who uses their products.

05_prototype_design_system.label: 05 — Prototype & Design System
05_prototype_design_system.heading: From AI brainstorm to production-ready screens
body: Instead of jumping straight into pixel-perfect mockups, I started with an AI-based prototype to quickly test concepts, layouts, and interaction patterns. This let me brainstorm and validate what works and what doesn't — before investing time in high-fidelity design.
Once the structure was proven, I built a scalable design system with reusable components, tokens, and patterns — then created the final mockups for the dev team.

ai_prototype.intro: Used AI tools to rapidly generate and iterate on layout concepts, testing different approaches to search, filtering, data cards, and access flows. This phase was about speed and exploration — finding the right direction before committing to a design system.

prompts.prompt: Generate a data marketplace homepage layout with: a prominent search bar at the top for natural language queries, a left sidebar with category filters (data type, domain, certification status), and a main grid of data product cards showing name, owner, quality score, and access status. Use a clean editorial style with generous whitespace. Focus on information density without clutter — engineers should scan 20+ items without fatigue.

Design a detail page for a single data product in a data marketplace. Include: a top header with product name, domain badge, quality score indicator, certification status, and access level chip; a tabbed body with Overview, Schema, Sample Data, and Lineage sections; a persistent right sidebar with metadata (owner, last updated, row count, format, sensitivity level) and a primary CTA button. The Overview tab should show description, use cases, and update frequency. The Schema tab should display a table of column names, data types, nullable flag, and descriptions. Use a clean, information-dense layout — this is a technical page for engineers who need to evaluate a dataset quickly.

Design a multi-step publishing form for adding a new data product to a data marketplace. Step 1 — Basic info: product name, short description, domain selector, data format, thumbnail upload. Step 2 — Schema: auto-import schema from Databricks with a column list showing name, type, and nullable; allow editors to add descriptions and toggle sensitivity flags per column. Step 3 — Ownership and access: assign a Data Product Owner from an AD-synced user list, set default access level (Open / Restricted / Private), add editors by team or individual. Include a sticky preview panel on the right showing how the product card will appear in the marketplace. Keep the flow approachable for non-technical DPOs who don't work directly in Databricks.

Design an access request modal for a data marketplace. The form should include: requester name and role (pre-filled from profile), team and POD, domain, requested access level (Viewer or Editor), target environment (Development or Production), and a mandatory justification field with a 50-character minimum. Show a read-only summary of the requested data product on the right side of the modal. On submission, display a confirmation screen with a unique request ID, the assigned reviewer's name, and an estimated review timeline. Ensure the form validates all required fields inline before allowing submission.

ai_prototype.caption: Multiple iterations across layout, search, filtering, and access flow concepts — tested structurally before any high-fidelity work began.
ai_prototype.prototype_note: Interactive prototype protected under NDA. Available on request.

token_architecture.intro: One system, two themes, zero duplication. Every color in the product traces through three layers — primitives at the base, semantic tokens that swap between light and dark modes, and component tokens that apply them consistently.
token_architecture.token_count: ~158 primitive · ~45 semantic · ~459 component tokens across 2 themes

nda_rows: Final mockups — Protected under NDA, available on request

06_validation.label: 06 — Validation
06_validation.heading: Testing with the people who'd actually use it
06_validation.intro: Before moving to development, I ran two structured feedback sessions with the primary user groups — validating designs, aligning on roles and workflows, and catching blind spots early.

sessions.title: Session 1 — Data POD Leads
sessions.description: Design review & feedback session with Data POD Leads — the technical owners who create and maintain data products on the internal data platform. Focused on validating roles, publishing workflows, and access management.
key_takeaways: Clear separation between POD Lead and Data Product Owner responsibilities
Dev access management remains an open exploration topic

sessions.title: Session 2 — Data Product Owners
sessions.description: Design review & feedback session with Data Product Owners — the business owners of data products, responsible for final approval and publication decisions. Focused on publication flow, access governance, and role alignment.
key_takeaways: Clear separation between technical and business ownership
Access management should be user-centric with clear access reasons
Publication and access decisions should stay lightweight
Marketplace enables confident decisions without exposing technical complexity

07_handoff.label: 07 — Hand-off to Dev
07_handoff.heading: AI-powered design-to-code pipeline
07_handoff.intro: Traditional hand-off means screenshots, redlines, and a PDF that's out of date the moment you save it. On this project, I tried something else: connect developers directly to the Figma design system through MCP — Model Context Protocol — so they could pull component specs, tokens, and interaction states from their IDE in real time. No more guessing from static docs.

traditional: Components drift from source over time
mcp: Live component specs pulled from Figma
Dev queries the design system from the IDE
One source of truth, always current

07_handoff.code_example:
// Component generated via MCP pull from Figma
<Button
  variant="primary"
  size="md"
  background="var(--color-primary-background)"  // → Figma: primary-background
  color="var(--color-primary-foreground)"        // → Figma: primary-foreground
  radius="var(--radius-md)"                      // → Figma: radius-md
>
  Request access
</Button>
07_handoff.code_caption: Generic example — structure shown, real component names under NDA.

how_mcp_worked.detail: MCP server connected to Figma file, exposing the design system as structured data that dev tools could consume in real time.
Developers accessed component specs, props, variants, and usage guidelines directly from their IDE — no context switching to Figma.
Design tokens (colors, typography, spacing, elevation) synced automatically — any update in Figma propagated to code.
Significantly reduced back-and-forth between design and dev. Components were built accurately on the first pass, matching the design system 1:1.

what_made_it_successful.detail: MCP reads layer names, frame structure, and component hierarchy. A well-organized Figma file with clear section naming made the design system legible to the AI — messy files produce messy output.
Three-tier token architecture (primitives → semantic → component) gave MCP a stable interface. Any token change in Figma propagated cleanly to code.
Dev and design aligned on component names, prop names, and variant terminology up front. Naming consistency turned out to be the biggest unlock.
Fewer back-and-forth cycles meant design could iterate on edge cases while dev built the core in parallel.

07_handoff.result_note: Result: components delivered significantly faster than traditional handoff cycles — fewer iterations, higher first-pass accuracy.

08_post_launch_research.label: 08 — Post-launch Research
08_post_launch_research.heading: Launch isn't the finish line — measuring what's next
08_post_launch_research.intro: The platform is currently in active development and heading toward launch. Rather than waiting until go-live to figure out how to measure success, I designed the post-launch research program upfront — combining traditional analytics, modern AI-powered tools, and continuous user feedback.

what_we_measure.detail: Active users, onboarding completion rates, and frequency of return visits across all persona groups.
Time-to-find for datasets, search-to-access conversion, and first-attempt success rate for access requests.
In-product micro-surveys and follow-up interviews triggered by key user actions.
Friction identified through AI-summarized session replays, support tickets, and heatmap analysis.

research_stack.detail: Natural language queries across usage data to spot trends and cohorts faster than manual dashboards.
LLM-generated insights from user sessions instead of watching hundreds of recordings manually.
Weekly short conversations with real users, built into the process rather than run as one-off studies.
Combining survey responses, support tickets, and interviews into themed insights automatically via LLM synthesis.

iteration_loop.detail: Collect feedback through multiple channels — surveys, session replays, interviews, and support tickets.
Theme the findings and prioritize by impact on user tasks and business goals.
Iterate on the most critical friction points in fast, focused cycles.
Re-measure to confirm the change actually moved the metric.

09_outcome.label: 09 — Outcome
09_outcome.heading: Built, validated, ready to ship
body: The platform is built, validated with real users, and handed off for final development. Launch is weeks away.

What's already in place: a three-tier design system with dev-integrated MCP pipeline, a two-phase UX strategy, two rounds of stakeholder validation, and a tested Figma-to-code workflow that's already producing components 1:1 with the design system.

10_reflection.label: 10 — Reflection
10_reflection.heading: What I learned along the way

columns.title: What I'd do differently
columns.body: Involve data governance stakeholders earlier in the process. Their requirements only became clear mid-project, which meant reworking parts of the access request flow that were already in motion.

columns.title: What this project taught me
columns.body: Leading a project end-to-end as the only designer taught me that seniority isn't about headcount — it's about owning decisions across the whole stack. And AI in the design process isn't magic, it's leverage — it speeds up the parts you already understand well, and it's useless for the parts you don't. The best outcomes came when I knew exactly what I wanted before asking for it.

columns.title: What I'm taking forward
columns.body: The Figma + MCP pipeline is going into every project from here. Once you've shipped components 1:1 with the design system on the first pass, traditional hand-off feels broken.

meta.role: Solo Lead UX + AI — discovery, design system, launch
meta.result: One designer, full product shipped in 14 weeks

dobrodoc.title: DobroDoc+
dobrodoc.hook: Redesign cut time-to-booking by 40% in usability tests

meta.role: Lead UX — user interviews, IA, mobile prototyping
meta.result: Redesign cut time-to-booking by 40% in usability tests

01_problem_context.label: 01 — Problem & Context
01_problem_context.heading: Booking was harder than the illness
body: DobroDoc+ already existed — but the original app was outdated and frustrating to use. Booking a doctor's appointment required more effort than the illness itself.

The core pain: when you're sick, the last thing you want is to navigate a broken interface or travel to a clinic just to get in the queue. Busy adults needed a way to see a doctor without leaving home.
The goal was a full redesign — faster booking, clearer navigation, and an experience that works when you feel your worst.

steps.title: UX Audit
steps.desc: Analysed existing app — broken flows, inconsistent patterns, key drop-off points.

steps.title: User Research
steps.desc: Interviewed busy adults about medical apps and clinic visits.

steps.title: UX Strategy
steps.desc: Defined redesign priorities. Goal: booking under 3 taps, eliminate redundant screens.

steps.title: Wireframes
steps.desc: Redesigned core flows — booking, doctor search, medical history.

steps.title: Usability Study
steps.desc: Two rounds — lo-fi and hi-fi — with busy adults.

steps.title: Mockups & Hi-fi
steps.desc: Full visual redesign with new component library.

03_strategy_research.label: 03 — Artifacts: UX Strategy
03_strategy_research.heading: Strategic foundation
03_strategy_research.vision: Accessible telemedicine platform for everyone, regardless of location or schedule
user_goals: Book without calling during business hours
Access medical history in one place

stakeholder_interviews.participants: Medical Director, COO, Head of Product, Compliance Officer

03_strategy_research.user_flow_summary: Overview flow covering 5 user scenarios: symptom search, appointment booking, video consultation, consultation history, and medical certificates.

04_mockups.label: 04 — Mockups
04_mockups.heading: Key screens in the redesign
04_mockups.intro: Final hi-fi mockups covering the core booking flow — from onboarding to appointment confirmation.
screens: Onboarding — create a medical card for yourself and your family
Home — clinic dashboard with doctor categories and quick actions
Doctor selection — choose med card, consultation type (online, in clinic, at home)
Confirmation — summary with med card, specialty, consultation type, date & time

rounds.title: Round 1 — Lo-fi prototype
findings: Users wanted doctor availability before clicking profiles
Symptom checker felt untrustworthy — no disclaimers
changes: Availability indicators added on doctor cards
Symptom checker redesigned with disclaimer + emergency warnings
Persistent family switcher added at top of screens

rounds.title: Round 2 — Hi-fi prototype
findings: Users wanted connection status before video call
100% of users loved digital prescription feature
Users wanted appointment reminders before consultations
changes: Connection quality indicator + test call added

comparisons.title: 01 — Doctor card
comparisons.after: Availability indicator — green dot + next free slot on card

comparisons.title: 02 — Symptom checker
comparisons.after: Medical disclaimer + emergency warning, AI match with doctor recommendation

comparisons.title: 03 — Family switcher
comparisons.after: Persistent switcher at top of every screen

comparisons.title: 04 — Pre-call screen
comparisons.after: Connection quality check + test call option

comparisons.title: 05 — Post-consultation
comparisons.after: Summary card in-app + auto email with diagnosis and prescription PDF

07_outcome.label: 07 — Outcome
07_outcome.heading: 40% faster booking
07_outcome.body: Redesign launched in late 2020. The updated app reduced time-to-booking by 40% in usability tests. Digital prescriptions and post-consultation summaries became the most praised features in early feedback.
07_outcome.status: The app is live on both platforms today.

columns.title: What I'd do differently
columns.body: Start with a design system from day one. Component inconsistencies between screens slowed the hi-fi phase and required cleanup before handoff.

columns.title: What I learned
columns.body: Two rounds of usability testing caught fundamentally different issues — lo-fi revealed structural problems, hi-fi surfaced emotional and trust concerns. Both are non-negotiable for a healthcare product.

dopas.title: dopas.dp.ua
dopas.hook: Designed from zero — platform launched and generating revenue

meta.result: Designed from zero — platform launched and generating revenue

01_problem_context.label: 01 — Problem & Context
01_problem_context.heading: No online option existed
body: Before dopas.dp.ua, bus tickets in Dnipro were sold exclusively offline — at ticket offices and directly from drivers. For a city with diverse ridership spanning all age groups, this meant queues, cash dependency, and no record of purchase.
The challenge: build an online ticketing platform from zero that works for everyone — from teenagers booking on a phone to elderly passengers unfamiliar with digital services.

steps.title: Market Analysis
steps.desc: Audited existing ticketing platforms in Ukraine and nearby markets.

steps.title: Wireframes & Lo-fi
steps.desc: Mapped core user flows — search, seat selection, checkout, ticket storage.

steps.title: Usability Study
steps.desc: Tested lo-fi prototype with users across age groups.

steps.title: Mockups & Hi-fi
steps.desc: Translated validated flows into high-fidelity screens.

steps.title: Testing & Iterating
steps.desc: Refined edge cases — returns, errors, empty states.

cards.title: Direct competitive audit
cards.desc: Reviewed 6 Ukrainian and regional bus ticketing platforms — Busfor, Infobus, Ukrzaliznytsia and others.

cards.title: Comparative analysis
cards.desc: Compared booking flows across competitors — search, seat selection, payment, post-purchase experience.

cards.title: Key insight
cards.desc: Most platforms forced 7+ steps before purchase. None offered ticket storage or self-service returns. This defined dopas's differentiators from day one.

04_user_flow.label: 04 — Artifacts: User Flow
04_user_flow.heading: End-to-end booking flow
04_user_flow.flow: Start → Open website → Search route → View results → Select trip? → [Yes] → Select seat → [No] → Return to search → Passenger info → Payment → Confirmation → Download ticket → End
04_user_flow.caption: Direction: End-to-end booking flow — from opening the website to downloading a ticket. Dashed line shows the return path when user decides not to purchase.

findings.issue: Seat selection too late in the flow
findings.detail: Users tried to go back mid-form to choose seats, causing confusion and drop-off.
findings.fix: Moved seat selection before passenger info

findings.detail: Multiple users abandoned the flow thinking the platform didn't support extra luggage.
findings.fix: Luggage toggle added on passenger info screen

findings.detail: Total cost including fees wasn't shown on results page — felt like hidden costs, reducing trust.
findings.fix: Full price shown on search results card

findings.detail: 3 of 5 users assumed one-way was the only option.
findings.fix: Round-trip toggle added to homepage search

06_wireframes.label: 06 — Wireframes
06_wireframes.heading: Defining the structure
06_wireframes.intro: Lo-fi wireframes defined the core structure before any visual design decisions were made.

screens.name: Homepage
screens.desc: Entry point. Search widget front and centre — destination, date, passenger count.

screens.name: Search results
screens.desc: Filterable list. Price, duration, and departure time visible without opening the ticket.

screens.name: Seat selection
screens.desc: Interactive bus map. Colour-coded by availability. One tap to select.

screens.name: Passenger info
screens.desc: Minimal form — only required fields. Luggage toggle added after usability study.

screens.name: Checkout
screens.desc: Full price breakdown and cancellation policy visible before payment.

comparisons.title: 01 — Homepage
comparisons.before: Search buried below fold, no round-trip option
comparisons.after: Search front and centre, round-trip toggle added

comparisons.title: 02 — Search results
comparisons.before: Price shown without fees, no filters
comparisons.after: Full price on card, filters by time and price

comparisons.title: 03 — Seat selection
comparisons.after: Moved before passenger info — matches mental model

comparisons.title: 05 — Checkout
comparisons.before: First time user sees total with fees

08_outcome.label: 08 — Outcome
08_outcome.heading: Live and generating revenue
body: Launched in late autumn 2020. Users responded positively — the platform filled a real gap in the local market where no online alternative existed before.
dopas.dp.ua has been live and generating revenue since launch.

columns.title: What I'd do differently
columns.body: Start with a design system from day one. Building components ad hoc slowed the hi-fi phase and created inconsistencies fixed during handoff.

columns.title: What I learned
columns.body: Usability testing is non-negotiable — even two sessions caught four critical flow issues that would have shipped as bugs. No amount of design intuition replaces watching a real user get stuck.

halliburton_artifacts.title: Research & Strategy Artifacts
halliburton_artifacts.subtitle: Data Marketplace · Halliburton · 2026
halliburton_artifacts.intro: A detailed look at the UX research, strategy documents, and design artifacts produced throughout the project.
halliburton_artifacts.nda_notice: This project is under NDA. Sensitive details are blurred. General process and approach are shown.

01_personas.heading: User groups & needs
01_personas.intro: Four persona groups were identified through stakeholder interviews and contextual research. Each represents a distinct relationship with data products in the organization.

personas.name: Data Scientist / Engineer
needs: Browse data products, fast and relevant search

personas.name: Business User
needs: Find existing reports (PBI, Tableau, Spotfire, Databricks)
Get answers to questions from trusted data
Won't use what isn't officially validated

personas.name: Data POD Lead
needs: Needs incident response tools and schema change alerts

02_jtbd.heading: What users are trying to accomplish
jobs_to_be_done: When I need specific data, I want to find it quickly so I can use it in my pipeline
When I find a dataset, I want to understand if it's trustworthy so I can decide whether to request access
When I request access, I want a fast approval so I don't lose time waiting
When I manage data products, I want clear tools so I can maintain quality and control access
core_user_scenarios: Search and discover relevant data products
Filter and narrow down data products
Open dataset detail page and understand if it's usable
Request and get 1-click access directly from dataset page

03_ux_goals_principles.heading: Design direction and guardrails
ux_goals: Validate whether users can find the right Data Product quickly
Check if users understand Data Product cards and details
Evaluate how easily users can request access
Validate clarity of access statuses and permissions
Test understanding of View vs Edit access levels
Validate that product owners can easily add existing products

04_roadmap.heading: Project phases
04_roadmap.intro: A seven-phase roadmap structured the project from initial discovery through post-launch iteration.

05_kpis.heading: Measuring success
05_kpis.intro: KPIs were defined across efficiency, adoption, quality, and business impact to track the marketplace's performance.
05_kpis.efficiency: Search time: weeks → minutes. Access request: months → days.
05_kpis.adoption: % engineers using marketplace ↑, % data products onboarded ↑
05_kpis.quality_satisfaction: CSAT ↑, % users finding right product on first search ↑

06_key_deliverables.heading: What was produced
06_key_deliverables.intro: A comprehensive set of UX research and design artifacts delivered across all project phases.

deliverables.title: UX Strategy & Roadmap
deliverables.desc: Long-term plan connecting user insights, product vision, and business objectives.

deliverables.title: Personas & JTBD
deliverables.desc: Four persona groups with detailed needs, goals, and jobs to be done.

deliverables.title: User Journey Maps & Userflows
deliverables.desc: End-to-end journey maps and interaction flows for all scenarios.

deliverables.title: Wireframes & Mockups
deliverables.desc: Lo-fi wireframes and hi-fi mockups for all core screens.

deliverables.title: Interactive Prototype
deliverables.desc: Clickable prototype used for stakeholder reviews and usability testing.

deliverables.title: Usability Testing Reports
deliverables.desc: Two rounds of formative and summative testing with synthesized findings.

deliverables.title: Scalable Design System
deliverables.desc: Component library built on corporate design system with custom marketplace elements.

deliverables.title: Post Launch Research Report
deliverables.desc: Usage analytics and research insights driving continuous improvement.

07_phase2_evolution.heading: From browsing to AI-powered discovery
07_phase2_evolution.intro: Phase 2 evolved the marketplace vision to incorporate trust signals, social knowledge, and AI-powered search.
07_phase2_evolution.vision: Transform the Data Marketplace from a browsing tool into an AI-powered discovery platform.
07_phase2_evolution.problem: Users can find data but cannot judge if it's reliable, relevant, or safe to use.
07_phase2_evolution.success: Engineers describe what they need in plain language, receive ranked trusted results in seconds, and make access decisions confidently.

features.name: AI Home
features.desc: Natural language input — user describes what they need. AI response + ranked dataset cards.

features.name: Recommendations
features.desc: 6 personalized boxes: Continue exploring, Most trusted, New since last visit, Pending action, Colleagues using, For you.

features.name: Trust
features.desc: Quality score, Certified badges, Lineage visualization, Approval workflow

features.name: Social
features.desc: Reviews & Usage tab, Peer recommendations, Owner reputation

features.name: AI Discovery
features.desc: NL search, Hybrid AI response, Personalized recommendations

features.name: Billing
features.desc: Subscription model, Plan comparison, Transparent pricing
